articles.author: Curtis Yee
articles.description: King Charles III will meet President Donald Trump and speak before Congress as he tries to emphasize the bond between the United Kingdom and the United States.
articles.title: Live updates: King Charles visits Washington with hopes of restoring the US-UK relationship

articles.author: Melissa Goldin
articles.description: Conspiracy theories flooded the internet minutes after the shooting at the White House Correspondents’ Dinner attended by President Donald Trump. The events Saturday played out in front of some of the nation’s most powerful reporters and editors, who snapped into action in real time to provide detailed accounts from the scene. What resulted was a steady stream of facts from myriad reputable media outlets. Despite this, unfounded conspiracy theories from both the left and the right proliferated, chief among them that the shooting was staged. Some spread in spite of the facts while others used real information to create false narratives.
articles.title: Why conspiracy theories spread at correspondents' dinner

articles.author: Bill Barrow
articles.description: Ron DeSantis is back in the national spotlight as he pushes for a new congressional map in Florida. His proposal aims to give Republicans four more seats, equivalent to the expected Democratic gains in Virginia. With his second term ending, this is a key moment for DeSantis to show he could still be a future national leader for his party. But his special session ran into a snag as soon as it opened Tuesday when Florida House leaders said they would not consider DeSantis' agenda on artificial intelligence regulations and relaxing school vaccine requirements. Some Republicans, meanwhile, worry the redistricting plan might backfire and help Democrats. As DeSantis navigates the session, his relationship with President Donald Trump and other Republican leaders will be on the line.
articles.title: Florida's redistricting fight puts DeSantis back in Republican spotlight

articles.author: Jon Gambrell
articles.description: The United Arab Emirates announced that it will leave OPEC effective May 1, stripping the oil cartel of one of its largest producers and further weakening its leverage over global oil supplies and prices. The UAE’s decision had been rumored as a possibility for some time, as it pushed back in recent years against OPEC production quotas it felt had been too low. Regional politics are also likely at play. The UAE has had increasingly frosty relations with Saudi Arabia, OPEC’s largest producer, over political and economic matters in the Mideast, even after both came under attack by fellow OPEC member Iran during the war.
articles.title: UAE says it will leave OPEC, a blow to the oil cartel

articles.author: Brian P. D. Hannon, Laurie Kellman
articles.description: The Trump administration seemed unlikely Tuesday to accept Iran's offer to end the war and reopen the Strait of Hormuz if the U.S. lifts its blockade on the country.
articles.title: Live updates: US appears cold to Iranian proposal to end the war without nuclear deal

articles.author: Stan Choe
articles.description: Weakening AI stocks and another climb in oil prices are helping to halt Wall Street’s record-setting rally. The S&P 500 slipped 0.4% Tuesday from its latest all-time high. The Dow Jones Industrial Average, which has less of an emphasis on technology stocks, held up better and rose 32 points, while the Nasdaq composite fell 0.7%. Nvidia, whose chips are powering much of the AI revolution, was the heaviest weight on the S&P 500. Oil prices rose by more than 2% as uncertainty continues on when the Strait of Hormuz could reopen and allow oil tankers to leave the Persian Gulf again.
articles.title: Sinking AI stocks and rising oil prices weigh on Wall Street

articles.author: Amir-hussein Radjy, Lee Keath, Sarah El Deeb
articles.description: U.S. and Israeli airstrikes crippled thousands of factories in Iran, and the damage is reverberating across the country's economy. Hundreds of thousands of Iranians have lost their jobs, and millions more could face the same fate. Most damaging, Israeli strikes knocked out most production of steel and petrochemicals, causing a surge in prices for metals and plastic and threatening wider sectors. Things could get worse under the U.S. blockade. Still, Iran's leaders are betting that an economy built to be self-reliant under decades of sanctions can endure the pain longer than U.S. President Donald Trump.
articles.title: Israel's strikes and Trump's blockade have battered Iran's economy

articles.author: Claire Galofaro, Sally Ho
articles.description: An Associated Press investigation finds that a business known for tough-love boarding schools for rebellious, rich teenagers has set its sights on a different demographic: adopted kids. Experts say adoptees account for an estimated 25-40% of those in residential treatment. What some call the “troubled teen industry,” a sprawling network of loosely regulated, for-profit residential treatment centers and boarding schools advertise to adoptive parents, promising to help adoptees heal, at a cost as high as $20,000 a month. Adoptees told AP they believe were in a shadow orphanage system where children end up institutionalized in oppressive, sometimes abusive facilities.
articles.title: AP investigation: Adopted kids confined in private for-profit facilities

articles.author: Mark Vancleave
articles.description: Federal agents have served search warrants in Minnesota in an ongoing fraud investigation of publicly funded social programs for children. Few details were released, though agents could be seen outside childcare centers in the Minneapolis area on Tuesday. Gov. Tim Walz welcomed the action. Minnesota was the site of an immigration crackdown that led to the deaths of two people this year. Before that crackdown, the government brought charges against dozens of people, many of them Somali Americans, who were charged with fleecing a federal program that was meant to provide food to kids.
articles.title: Federal agents serve search warrants in Minnesota fraud probe

articles.author: Barbara Ortutay
articles.description: OpenAI co-founders Elon Musk and Sam Altman have shown up for Tuesday’s opening statements in a high-stakes trial revolving around a bitter feud between the former friends that could reshape the future development of artificial intelligence. The bickering billionaires’ early-morning appearances at the Oakland, California, federal courthouse foreshadow what could be a dramatic start to a legal drama that is expected to be brimming with intrigue and potentially embarrassing details about the two tech moguls. The jury was elected Monday and the trial is scheduled to take three weeks.
articles.title: Musk, Altman appear for opening statements in trial over OpenAI's origins
